proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the catalyst surface as a dynamic ensemble of non-equilibrium states, analogous to protein folding, and applying a suite of operando techniques to capture this 'surface folding' landscape in real time, rather than inferring activity from pre- or post-reaction characterization.

proposals.title: Electrochemical C-C Coupling on Coordination-Locked Cu-N4 Sites: Tuning Selectivity via Matrix-Derived Electronic Perturbation
proposals.motivation: Electrochemical C-C coupling is a critical step for sustainable chemical synthesis from CO2 or biomass-derived feedstocks, but achieving high selectivity and activity remains a challenge. Single-atom catalysts (SACs) offer unique, well-defined active sites, yet their instability under reductive or thermal conditions leads to aggregation and deactivation, obscuring fundamental structure-activity relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthesizing Cu SACs via pyrolysis of a Cu-ZIF precursor will create thermally stable, coordination-locked Cu-N4 sites within a nitrogen-doped carbon matrix, and that the electronic structure of these sites can be systematically tuned by secondary heteroatom doping to control the selectivity of electrochemical C-C coupling reactions.
proposals.method: Catalysts will be synthesized via pyrolysis (600-1000°C) of a Cu-doped ZIF-8 precursor under inert atmosphere to form a Cu-N-C matrix. Secondary heteroatoms (B, P) will be introduced via co-pyrolysis with boric acid or triphenylphosphine. Materials will be characterized using HAADF-STEM, XPS, and XAS to confirm atomic dispersion and coordination. Electrochemical testing for C-C coupling (e.g., electroreduction of furfural to hydrofuroin) will be performed in a continuous-flow electrochemical cell with online product analysis via GC/MS, enabling direct correlation of activity/selectivity with the electronic properties derived from spectroscopic descriptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, aggregation-resistant SAC platform for studying electrochemical surface reactions under realistic conditions. It will deliver a fundamental understanding of how matrix-induced electronic perturbations in M-N4 sites influence binding energies and reaction pathways for multi-carbon product formation, providing design principles beyond Cu for coupling reactions.
proposals.novelty: While Cu-N-C SACs are known for ORR or CO2 reduction to C1 products, their deliberate stabilization and electronic tuning for selective *electrochemical* C-C bond formation is unexplored. The approach integrates Nørskov-group expertise in electrochemical reaction analysis with a materials synthesis strategy designed to freeze the active site geometry.

proposals.title: Electrochemically Allosteric Single-Atom Alloy Catalysts for Tunable C-C Coupling
proposals.motivation: Electrochemical C-C coupling is a critical step in sustainable fuel and chemical synthesis, but achieving high selectivity remains a major challenge. Single-atom alloy (SAA) catalysts offer precise active sites, but they lack the dynamic, responsive control over reaction pathways that enzymes achieve through allosteric regulation. Integrating this biological control principle into heterogeneous electrochemistry could unlock unprecedented selectivity by enabling real-time, external modulation of the catalytic site.
proposals.hypothesis: The electronic structure and catalytic activity of a single copper atom embedded in a host metal (e.g., Pd, Au) can be dynamically and reversibly modulated by applying an electrochemical potential to deliberately engineered 'regulatory' features, such as a distant vacancy or a secondary dopant atom, thereby controlling the selectivity of electrochemical C-C coupling reactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize model SAA surfaces (e.g., Cu/Pd(111)) via physical vapor deposition and controlled annealing, confirmed by scanning tunneling microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Regulatory features (e.g., subsurface vacancies, co-doped S atoms) will be engineered at defined distances from the Cu site. Using a combination of in situ electrochemical scanning tunneling microscopy and synchrotron-based ambient pressure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, we will correlate applied potential with changes in the Cu site's electronic density of states and adsorption geometry. Density functional theory calculations, including explicit solvation and field effects, will simulate the potential-dependent electronic coupling between the regulatory feature and the Cu active site for model reactions like CO dimerization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for 'allosteric' heterogeneous catalysts with externally tunable activity, moving beyond static catalyst design. It will provide fundamental mechanistic insight into how long-range electronic effects in alloys can be harnessed for electrochemical control, directly contributing to the rational design of catalysts for complex, multi-step electrosynthesis.
proposals.novelty: While SAAs are known, the concept of designing a distinct, non-participating 'regulatory' site to dynamically control a single-atom active site via an electrochemical stimulus is unprecedented. This directly transposes a core principle of enzymology—allosteric regulation—into the realm of inorganic electrocatalysis.

proposals.title: In-situ Electrochemical Stabilization of Cu Single-Atom Alloys on Sulfur-Vacancy Engineered MoS2 for Durable CO2 Reduction to C2+ Products
proposals.motivation: Electrochemical CO2 reduction to valuable multi-carbon (C2+) products is a critical route for sustainable chemical synthesis, but suffers from catalyst instability and poor selectivity. Single-atom catalysts (SACs) offer high atom efficiency and unique selectivity, yet they are prone to aggregation under reductive potentials, especially for demanding C-C coupling reactions. A method to dynamically stabilize isolated metal atoms during operation is a major unsolved challenge in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: Electrochemically generated and maintained sulfur vacancies in the basal plane of 2H-MoS2 will serve as stable, well-defined anchoring sites for isolated Cu atoms, creating a single-atom alloy (SAA) catalyst where the applied potential actively prevents Cu aggregation, thereby enabling durable and selective CO2 reduction to C2+ products.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phase-pure 2H-MoS2 nanosheets and use controlled electrochemical reduction in a non-aqueous electrolyte to generate a tunable density of sulfur vacancies. Cu will be deposited via galvanic displacement or electrochemical underpotential deposition. The catalyst will be characterized using in-situ Raman and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to monitor vacancy and Cu site stability under CO2RR conditions in a flow cell. Activity and selectivity for ethylene and ethanol will be measured via online gas chromatography and NMR, with stability tests exceeding 100 hours. Density functional theory (DFT) simulations will model vacancy formation energies and Cu adsorption/aggregation barriers under potential control.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel paradigm for creating electrochemically stabilized single-atom alloy catalysts, directly linking applied potential to atomic-scale site integrity. It will provide fundamental insights into the dynamic management of defects for catalyst durability and demonstrate a path toward regenerable, high-performance catalysts for complex multi-step electrocatalysis.
proposals.novelty: While sulfur vacancies in MoS2 are known to be active for HER, their use as electrochemically 'tunable' anchors to stabilize a non-precious SAA for C-C coupling is unexplored. The concept of using the operational potential not just to drive a reaction, but to actively maintain the catalyst's atomic structure against degradation, represents a significant departure from conventional static catalyst design.

proposals.title: Electrochemical C-C Coupling on Copper Single-Atom Alloy Catalysts: A Combined DFT and Microkinetic Study for C2+ Product Formation
proposals.motivation: The electrochemical reduction of CO2 to multi-carbon (C2+) products is a critical route to sustainable fuels and chemicals, but suffers from low selectivity and efficiency. Copper is the only pure metal known to catalyze C-C coupling at appreciable rates, yet its activity and selectivity require optimization. Single-atom alloys (SAAs), where isolated active sites are dispersed in a host metal, offer a promising platform to tune adsorption energetics and reaction pathways with atomic precision, potentially overcoming the limitations of pure Cu.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dispersing single Cu atoms within a suitable host metal (e.g., Ag, Au, or Pd) will create unique, isolated adsorption sites that selectively stabilize key C1 intermediates (e.g., *CO), while modulating the subsequent C-C coupling step (*CO dimerization or *COH insertion) to enhance the rate and selectivity towards ethylene or ethanol.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations, using the BEEF-vdW functional and explicit solvation models, to map the free energy landscape for CO2RR to C2+ products on a series of Cu SAAs. High-throughput screening of host metals and coordination environments will identify descriptors for C-C coupling activity. These results will inform the synthesis of model SAA surfaces via physical vapor deposition. The theoretical microkinetic models, benchmarked against experimental data from our continuous-flow electrochemical reactor system, will be used to predict and validate optimal catalyst compositions and operating potentials.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental, atomic-scale understanding of C-C coupling on isolated, well-defined sites, moving beyond ensemble effects in pure metals. It will provide a generalizable descriptor-based framework for designing SAA catalysts for multi-step electrochemical reactions and deliver specific catalyst candidates with predicted enhanced selectivity for C2+ products from CO2RR.
proposals.novelty: While SAAs have been studied for thermocatalytic reactions, their systematic application to the complex, multi-electron electrochemical C-C coupling problem is novel. This approach uniquely isolates the intrinsic activity of a single Cu site, decoupling it from the ensemble effects and varying coordination environments present in nanoparticles or alloys, which is a non-obvious strategy given the perceived need for contiguous Cu sites for coupling.

proposals.title: Mapping Morphology to Selectivity in Electrochemical CO2 Reduction: A Descriptor Landscape Approach for Copper Nanoparticle Design
proposals.motivation: The electrochemical reduction of CO2 on copper catalysts is a promising route to sustainable fuels and chemicals, but achieving high selectivity for a single C2+ product like ethylene remains a major challenge. Pre-2026 work has established that catalytic activity and selectivity are highly sensitive to nanoparticle morphology, but a predictive, quantitative framework linking specific structural features to reaction pathway branching is lacking. This gap hinders the rational design of next-generation catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the selectivity-determining step in C2+ formation on copper nanoparticles can be predicted by constructing a multi-dimensional catalytic descriptor landscape, where key electronic and geometric descriptors, computed for a library of morphologies, map directly to the energy barriers of competing C-C coupling pathways.
proposals.method: We will generate a vast library of Cu nanoparticle structures (size, shape, faceting) using Wulff construction and atomistic modeling. For each structure, we will compute established catalytic descriptors (generalized coordination number, d-band center, facet-specific *CO and *OCCO adsorption energies) using Density Functional Theory (DFT). These descriptors will serve as input features for a graph neural network (GNN) model, trained on a DFT-calculated dataset of activation barriers for key C-C coupling steps leading to ethylene vs. ethanol precursors. The trained model will generate a high-dimensional selectivity landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a predictive, visual framework that directly links catalyst morphology to reaction pathway selectivity, moving beyond single-descriptor correlations. It will provide concrete design principles—such as optimal facet exposure and coordination environments—for synthesizing Cu nanoparticles with tailored selectivity. The descriptor landscape approach establishes a generalizable methodology for catalyst discovery beyond CO2 reduction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the concept of descriptor-based binding landscapes from computational biochemistry to heterogeneous electrocatalysis, creating a unified, multi-dimensional map for product selectivity. This goes beyond traditional linear scaling relations by using a machine-learned landscape to capture the complex, non-linear interplay of multiple structural descriptors governing pathway bifurcation.

proposals.title: First-Principles Microkinetic Modeling of Hydrogen Spillover as a Dynamic Control for Selectivity in Ammonia Oxidation and Oxygen Reduction Reactions
proposals.motivation: Controlling selectivity in thermal catalytic networks involving hydrogen, such as ammonia oxidation to N2 vs. NOx or selective oxygen reduction, remains a grand challenge. While hydrogen spillover is a recognized phenomenon, its role is often qualitative, and a predictive, quantitative framework linking spillover flux to product distribution is lacking. Integrating this concept with first-principles microkinetics would bridge the gap between atomic-scale catalyst design and reactor-level performance optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the flux of spilled-over hydrogen acts as a dynamic, tunable variable that dictates the relative rates of hydrogenation and dehydrogenation pathways on support sites, thereby governing overall product selectivity in reactions like ammonia oxidation. This control can be predicted by a microkinetic model that explicitly incorporates spillover as a parameter.
proposals.method: We will employ Density Functional Theory (DFT) to calculate activation barriers and binding energies for all elementary steps of target reactions (e.g., NH3 oxidation) on defined metal (e.g., Ir, Pt) and metal-oxide support (e.g., TiO2, Al2O3) surfaces. These parameters will populate a mean-field microkinetic model where the flux of atomic hydrogen from the metal to the support (spillover) is an explicit, variable input. The model will be solved using the CatMAP software package to simulate steady-state turnover frequencies and selectivity as a function of spillover efficiency, temperature, and pressure.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind computational platform to quantitatively predict how catalyst architecture (metal-support interface) and conditions govern selectivity via hydrogen spillover. It will provide specific design principles for catalyst-support pairs to maximize desired products, directly informing synthesis efforts for selective oxidation and reduction catalysts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the quantitative integration of spillover flux as a direct, tunable input into a first-principles microkinetic model, moving beyond descriptive roles of spillover to create a predictive tool for selectivity engineering. This approach explicitly connects the kinetics on disparate active sites (metal and support) through a controlled mass transfer variable.

proposals.title: Microkinetic Modeling of Electrochemical Ammonia Synthesis: Resolving the Potential-Dependent Rate-Determining Step via Field-Stabilized Intermediates
proposals.motivation: The electrochemical nitrogen reduction reaction (eNRR) to ammonia is a promising route for sustainable fertilizer production, but its practical efficiency remains low due to poor selectivity and competing hydrogen evolution. Pre-2026 computational studies often rely on simplified thermodynamic descriptors or single-step barriers, failing to capture the complex, potential-dependent interplay of multiple proton-electron transfer steps under realistic electrochemical conditions. A rigorous, first-principles microkinetic framework is needed to bridge this gap and guide catalyst design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for eNRR on transition metal oxides, the rate-determining step shifts under applied potential due to the electric-field stabilization of key nitrogen-containing intermediates (e.g., *N2H or *NH2), and that this shift critically controls the Faradaic efficiency versus hydrogen evolution.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) with explicit solvation and a constant potential grand-canonical framework to compute adsorption energies and activation barriers for all elementary steps in eNRR and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) on IrO2 and RuO2 surfaces. The computational hydrogen electrode will be extended using the double-reference method to model potential effects. These data will feed into a microkinetic model solved using the CatMAP software package, incorporating field effects on activation energies as derived from recent work on OER, to predict current densities and selectivities as a function of applied potential and electrolyte pH.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first fully potential-resolved microkinetic model for eNRR on oxide catalysts, quantitatively predicting activity-selectivity trade-offs. It will establish a general computational protocol for moving beyond scaling relations in complex electrocatalysis by integrating field effects into kinetic simulations, directly informing experimental synthesis and electrolyte process optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of electric field-dependent activation barriers, recently demonstrated for OER, into a comprehensive microkinetic model for the multi-step eNRR network, enabling the prediction of potential-dependent mechanistic shifts that are inaccessible to standard thermodynamic analysis.

proposals.title: Operando Stability and Degradation Pathways of SrCoO2.7 as an Oxygen Evolution Catalyst in Acidic Electrolyte
proposals.motivation: The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is a critical bottleneck for efficient water electrolysis and renewable fuel synthesis. While SrCoO2.7 (brownmillerite phase) has shown exceptionally high intrinsic OER activity in alkaline media, its practical application is hindered by unknown stability, particularly under the harsh, acidic conditions preferred for high-performance integrated electrolyzers. A systematic evaluation of its long-term structural integrity is essential to assess its viability as a non-precious metal catalyst.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SrCoO2.7 will undergo a time-dependent surface reconstruction and Sr leaching under anodic potentials in acid, leading to a decay in OER activity that correlates with the formation of a cobalt-rich, amorphous oxide layer.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phase-pure SrCoO2.7 thin films via pulsed laser deposition on conductive substrates for controlled studies. Long-term potentiostatic electrolysis will be performed in 0.5 M H2SO4, with periodic activity monitoring via cyclic voltammetry. Operando and post-mortem characterization will combine inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) of the electrolyte to quantify dissolution, with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) to track chemical, phase, and morphological evolution. Density functional theory (DFT) simulations will model surface termination stability and leaching energetics under potential control.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive stability profile and degradation mechanism map for a promising high-activity OER perovskite in acidic media. It will provide critical design rules for stabilizing cobalt-based oxides, informing future synthesis strategies like cation doping or protective overlayers. The combined operando experimental and theoretical approach will serve as a blueprint for assessing the durability of metastable, high-activity catalyst phases.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 studies focus on SrCoO2.7's activity, a dedicated investigation of its stability under technologically relevant, long-term acidic operation is lacking. The proposed work uniquely links real-time electrochemical performance decay with atomic-scale structural and compositional changes, moving beyond simple activity metrics.

proposals.title: Descriptor-Driven Design of Perovskite Oxides for the Acidic Oxygen Reduction Reaction via High-Throughput Computation and Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is the critical, rate-limiting cathode process in proton-exchange membrane fuel cells. Current catalysts rely on scarce platinum-group metals, and developing active, stable, and earth-abundant alternatives, particularly for the acidic electrolyte, remains a grand challenge. Perovskite oxides (ABO3) are promising candidates, but their optimization has been hindered by a lack of predictive, electronic-structure-based descriptors linking composition to ORR activity and stability under operating conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the activity and stability descriptors (e.g., *OH/*O adsorption energies, B-site d-band center, O 2p-band center, and metal-oxygen covalency) successfully developed for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) on perovskites can be systematically inverted and extended to predict and optimize materials for the thermodynamically opposite ORR.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) to perform high-throughput screening of doped perovskite libraries (e.g., LaBO3, B = Mn, Co, Fe with Sr, Ba, or vacancy doping), calculating established OER descriptors and their correlations to ORR intermediates (*OOH, *O, *OH) binding. Machine learning models (e.g., kernel ridge regression or graph neural networks) will be trained on these datasets to identify optimal descriptor combinations and predict new compositions. Promising candidates will be synthesized via continuous hydrothermal flow synthesis and combinatorial pulsed laser deposition, followed by electrochemical characterization in a three-electrode cell with rotating disk electrode to validate activity and stability in acidic electrolyte.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, descriptor-based framework for the rational design of perovskite ORR catalysts, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will yield specific, predicted catalyst compositions with validated performance, contributing a new materials database for acidic ORR. Furthermore, it will provide fundamental insight into the symmetry and scaling relations between OER and ORR on oxide surfaces.
proposals.novelty: While descriptor-based design is established for OER, its systematic, inverse application to ORR on perovskites in acid is novel. The explicit coupling of high-throughput DFT screening of inverted OER descriptors with advanced, continuous-flow synthesis methods for targeted perovskite validation represents a distinct, closed-loop computational-experimental approach.

proposals.title: Unifying Descriptor for Electrochemical Fuel Synthesis: Extending the OER Volcano to HER on Perovskite Oxides
proposals.motivation: The electrochemical oxygen evolution reaction (OER) on perovskite oxides is well-described by the occupancy of the e_g orbital of surface transition-metal cations, forming a volcano relationship. However, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), the complementary half-cell for fuel synthesis (e.g., in ammonia electrosynthesis via hydrogen oxidation), lacks a similarly robust and predictive electronic descriptor on these oxide surfaces. This gap hinders the rational design of bifunctional or selective catalysts for integrated renewable fuel processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hydrogen adsorption free energy (ΔG_H*), the conventional HER descriptor on metals, is governed by the same electronic structure features (e.g., e_g or t_2g occupancy, metal-oxygen covalency) that control the OER activity on perovskite oxides, thereby establishing a unified theoretical framework for both fuel-forming and fuel-consuming reactions.
proposals.method: We will employ Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using the BEEF-vdW functional to compute ΔG_H* on a series of ABO_3 perovskite (001) surfaces (e.g., LaBO_3, B = Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni). The electronic structure descriptors (e.g., e_g occupancy, O p-band center) will be extracted from the projected density of states. These will be correlated with ΔG_H* to construct a predictive model. The computational trends will be validated against experimental HER activity data from high-throughput synthesis of epitaxial perovskite thin films tested in a continuous-flow electrochemical cell with a non-aqueous electrolyte to suppress competing water reduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish, for the first time, a predictive electronic descriptor for HER on perovskite oxides, directly linking it to the established OER framework. It will provide a fundamental principle for designing oxide catalysts where both hydrogen and oxygen reaction pathways are critical, such as in electrosynthesis loops or integrated solar-fuel devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating HER not as an isolated process on metals but as an oxide electronic structure problem, testing if the powerful descriptor paradigm for OER is a special case of a more general principle governing surface electrocatalysis on perovskites.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Computational Screening of Alloy Catalysts for Selective Electrochemical CO2 Reduction to C2+ Products
proposals.motivation: The electrochemical reduction of CO2 to valuable multi-carbon (C2+) products like ethylene and ethanol offers a promising route to store renewable energy and close the carbon cycle. However, the process suffers from low selectivity and high overpotentials due to the complexity of the reaction network and the lack of efficient, stable catalysts. Pre-2026, the discovery of such catalysts remains a major bottleneck, primarily driven by Edisonian trial-and-error approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high-throughput density functional theory (DFT) screening methodologies, successfully developed for petrochemical processes like methane reforming and ammonia synthesis, can be adapted to systematically identify novel bimetallic alloy surfaces that stabilize key *CO-CO dimerization intermediates, thereby enhancing the selectivity towards C2+ products in CO2 reduction.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput DFT calculations using the Atomic Simulation Environment (ASE) and the BEEF-vdW functional to compute adsorption energies of critical intermediates (*CO, *CHO, *COH, *OCCO) across a vast library of bimetallic alloy surfaces (e.g., Cu-based, Ag-based). The computational hydrogen electrode model will be used to construct free energy diagrams at relevant potentials. Machine learning models (e.g., graph neural networks) will be trained on initial DFT data to predict adsorption energies and accelerate the screening. Promising candidates will be evaluated using microkinetic models for activity and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a curated, open database of adsorption energies for CO2RR intermediates on alloy surfaces and identify 3-5 previously unreported, promising catalyst compositions for experimental validation. It will establish a transferable computational workflow for electrocatalyst discovery, moving the field beyond copper-centric studies. The insights into descriptor-based design (e.g., *CO binding energy as a predictor for C-C coupling) will provide fundamental guidelines for catalyst engineering.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput screening is mature in thermocatalysis, its rigorous application to the complex, potential-dependent electrochemical CO2 reduction network, with explicit consideration of C-C coupling steps on alloy surfaces, is a novel and non-trivial extension. This approach moves beyond simple *CO or *H scaling relations to target the specific bottleneck step for multi-carbon formation.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Ternary Alloy Catalysts for Selective CO2 Reduction to C2+ Products via Combinatorial Synthesis and Electrochemical Screening
proposals.motivation: The electrochemical reduction of CO2 to valuable multi-carbon (C2+) products like ethylene and ethanol offers a route to sustainable chemical feedstocks and energy storage. However, the process suffers from low selectivity and high overpotentials due to the lack of efficient and stable catalysts. Pre-2026, the search for such catalysts has been slow, relying on the serial synthesis and testing of individual materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying combinatorial synthesis methods to create dense libraries of ternary alloy thin films, we can rapidly identify novel, non-intuitive catalyst compositions that exhibit high Faradaic efficiency for C2+ products at lower overpotentials than known Cu-based catalysts.
proposals.method: We will employ magnetron sputtering in a combinatorial gradient mode to fabricate thin-film material libraries, focusing on ternary systems based on Cu, Ag, and post-transition metals (e.g., In, Sn, Bi). These libraries will be integrated into a high-throughput electrochemical screening platform using a scanning droplet cell. Each library spot will be characterized in a CO2-saturated alkaline electrolyte (e.g., 0.1 M KHCO3) via automated cyclic voltammetry and potentiostatic tests. Product analysis will be performed using on-line mass spectrometry (for gases) and ex-situ NMR/GC for liquid products, with data managed via a custom automation and analysis pipeline.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rapid discovery pipeline for CO2 reduction catalysts, directly yielding new, high-performance alloy compositions. It will generate a comprehensive dataset linking composition, structure, and catalytic performance, providing fundamental insights into the design principles for multi-carbon formation. The methodology will be directly transferable to other electrochemical reactions, such as nitrogen reduction.
proposals.novelty: While combinatorial methods are established in materials science, their systematic application to the discovery of *electrocatalysts* for the complex, multi-step CO2-to-C2+ reaction remains limited. This approach uniquely combines gradient alloy synthesis with high-throughput *product-resolved* electrochemical screening, moving beyond simple activity proxies to directly map composition against product selectivity.

proposals.title: Beyond the Sabatier Principle: A Multi-Site Microkinetic Model for Hydrogen Adsorption in Electrochemical Ammonia Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Computational models for electrochemical nitrogen reduction (eNRR) to ammonia predominantly rely on the assumption of a single, identical adsorption site for key intermediates like *H, limiting their predictive power. This simplification fails to capture the heterogeneity of real catalyst surfaces, such as high-index facets, defects, and dopant environments, which are known to influence activity and selectivity. A more realistic model is needed to bridge the gap between idealized computational descriptors and experimental performance, particularly for complex processes like eNRR where hydrogen binding energy is a critical but oversimplified activity descriptor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the overall activity and selectivity of eNRR catalysts are governed by the statistical distribution and interplay of multiple, non-equivalent hydrogen adsorption sites, and that a microkinetic model incorporating this site heterogeneity will provide superior correlation with experimental data than single-site models.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-site microkinetic model by integrating density functional theory (DFT) calculations with kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulations. First, we will use DFT to map the distribution of hydrogen adsorption free energies on realistic catalyst models (e.g., stepped Ru surfaces, doped MoS2) derived from synthesis conditions. This distribution will serve as input for a kMC framework that explicitly simulates the competitive adsorption and reaction of *H, *N, and *NHx species across different site types. The model will be parameterized and validated against experimental activity and selectivity data from our continuous-flow electrosynthesis systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more accurate computational framework for understanding and predicting eNRR catalyst performance, moving beyond the single-descriptor paradigm. It will directly explain why certain synthesized materials outperform idealized models and offer guidelines for designing catalysts with optimal site distributions. The methodology will be broadly applicable to other multi-step electrochemical reactions, such as oxygen evolution, where site heterogeneity is equally critical.
proposals.novelty: While site heterogeneity is acknowledged, its explicit, quantitative integration into predictive microkinetic models for electrochemical ammonia synthesis is lacking. This approach uniquely combines detailed surface site characterization from DFT with the statistical power of kMC to model ensemble behavior, a significant advance over mean-field or single-site approximations common in the field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Predictive Descriptors for Hydrogen Electrochemistry on Bimetallic Alloys: Linking Surface Composition to Adsorption and Exchange Currents
proposals.motivation: The rational design of bimetallic alloy electrocatalysts for reactions like the hydrogen evolution/oxidation reaction (HER/HOR) and the nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) is hindered by an incomplete understanding of how alloying modifies key adsorption energetics and kinetic barriers. While the d-band model and scaling relations for pure metals are well-established, their predictive power for binary alloys, where ensemble and ligand effects dominate, remains less tested, especially for electrochemical proton-coupled electron transfer kinetics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hydrogen adsorption free energy (ΔG_H*) and the electrochemical exchange current density for the HER/HOR on transition metal alloys can be predicted by a small set of electronic and geometric descriptors derived from the local composition of the active site, moving beyond simple linear interpolation of pure metal properties.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations to compute ΔG_H* on a library of bimetallic surface models (e.g., Pt-, Pd-, Ir-, Ru-based alloys with 3d metals). Using the computational hydrogen electrode model, we will calculate adsorption energies on various surface sites (top, bridge, hollow) with different local compositions. These will be correlated with electronic descriptors (e.g., d-band center, Bader charge) and geometric descriptors (e.g., strain, coordination number). The resulting ΔG_H* trends will be used as input into microkinetic models based on the Butler-Volmer formalism to predict exchange current densities, which will be validated against experimental data from high-throughput synthesis and electrochemical characterization in controlled electrolyte environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, descriptor-based framework for predicting the hydrogen electrochemistry of alloy surfaces, directly linking atomic-scale structure to macroscopic kinetic rates. It will provide fundamental insights into how alloying tunes activity beyond the pure metal volcano peak, offering concrete design principles for next-generation catalysts for energy conversion processes like ammonia electrosynthesis via NRR and efficient hydrogen cycling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly moving from pure-metal scaling relations to a local-site descriptor approach for alloys and directly connecting these descriptors to electrochemical kinetics (exchange currents), a step beyond typical thermodynamic adsorption studies, thereby bridging a critical gap between surface science and applied electrochemistry.

proposals.title: Microkinetic Modeling of the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction: Bridging Surface Science and Electrochemical Performance
proposals.motivation: The hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is a cornerstone of electrochemical energy conversion, yet its detailed reaction network, especially under realistic electrochemical conditions, remains incompletely described. Current models often rely on simplified descriptors like the hydrogen adsorption free energy (ΔG_H*), which fail to capture the full potential-dependent kinetics and the influence of the electrolyte interface. A rigorous, first-principles microkinetic model, a standard tool in thermal heterogeneous catalysis, is critically needed to unify surface science insights with electrochemical performance for HER.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a detailed microkinetic model, incorporating potential-dependent activation barriers and explicit solvation effects, will accurately predict HER activity and Tafel slopes across different metal electrodes, revealing the dominant reaction mechanisms and rate-determining steps under operating conditions.
proposals.method: We will construct a first-principles microkinetic model for HER on Pt, Ir, and Ni surfaces. Activation barriers for elementary steps (Volmer, Heyrovsky, Tafel) will be calculated using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with an explicit solvation model and a charged interface, employing the computational hydrogen electrode to account for applied potential. The microkinetic network will be solved using the CatMAP software package, integrating these potential-dependent parameters to simulate polarization curves and extract Tafel slopes. The model will be validated against high-quality experimental data from the literature on well-defined single-crystal electrodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic framework for HER that moves beyond the Sabatier principle, explicitly linking atomic-scale surface properties to macroscopic electrochemical metrics. It will establish a generalizable methodology for applying heterogeneous catalysis microkinetic tools to electrochemical reactions, offering deeper insight into activity trends and mechanism transitions.
proposals.novelty: While microkinetic modeling is mature in thermal catalysis, its rigorous application to electrochemical HER, with full integration of potential-dependent energetics and interfacial solvation, represents a significant and non-trivial advancement. This approach goes beyond the prevailing descriptor-based models to capture the full kinetic network.

proposals.title: Beyond Hydrogen Adsorption: A Descriptor Space Analysis for Electrochemical Ammonia Synthesis via Nitrogen Reduction
proposals.motivation: The electrochemical nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) for sustainable ammonia synthesis is severely limited by poor activity and selectivity. The hydrogen adsorption energy (ΔG_H*), a successful descriptor for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), fails to capture the complex multi-step proton-electron transfer and N≡N bond activation in NRR. This necessitates the identification of new, more predictive electronic structure descriptors to accelerate catalyst discovery beyond the current trial-and-error paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of descriptors related to nitrogenous intermediates (e.g., *N2H, *NH2) and surface electronic properties (e.g., d-band center, work function, or orbital occupancy) will provide a superior activity/selectivity volcano for NRR, decoupling it from the competing HER.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) to compute a broad set of electronic and adsorption properties for a curated library of transition metal, alloy, and oxide surfaces relevant to NRR. Using high-throughput computational screening, we will calculate adsorption free energies for key NRR intermediates (*N2, *N2H, *NH2) and electronic descriptors (d-band center, Bader charges, crystal orbital Hamilton population). Machine learning models, specifically gradient-boosted trees and kernel ridge regression, will be trained to map these descriptors to the theoretical limiting potential, identifying the most predictive feature combinations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, multi-dimensional descriptor framework for NRR, moving beyond the single-descriptor paradigm. It will provide a rational design principle for NRR electrocatalysts and deliver a shortlist of promising candidate materials for experimental validation, specifically targeting enhanced selectivity against HER.
proposals.novelty: While descriptor analysis is established for reactions like OER, its systematic application to the more complex NRR landscape, with an explicit focus on moving beyond ΔG_H* and deconvoluting activity from selectivity, is a novel and critical advancement.

proposals.title: Microscopic Descriptors for the Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction: Extending the Sabatier Analysis from Oxygen Evolution
proposals.motivation: The hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) is the critical anode process in hydrogen fuel cells, yet its fundamental activity trends across metals are not fully understood. While the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) has been successfully rationalized using electronic structure descriptors like the *OH/*O adsorption energy, a similarly rigorous, predictive framework for HOR in aqueous electrolytes is lacking. Establishing such a framework is essential for rationally designing more active and cost-effective anode catalysts to replace platinum.
proposals.hypothesis: The HOR activity on transition metals in aqueous acid can be described by a single, universal descriptor derived from the binding energies of key reaction intermediates (e.g., *H, *OH), analogous to the scaling relations and volcano plots established for OER. We hypothesize that deviations from this simple Sabatier picture, particularly for Pt-group metals, can be explained by the influence of interfacial electric fields on the adsorption energetics.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations to compute the free energies of HOR intermediates (*H, *OH, *O, *OOH) on a range of pure transition metals and bimetallic surfaces. Scaling relations and theoretical volcano plots will be constructed using the adsorption energy of a primary intermediate (e.g., *H or *OH) as the descriptor. This analysis will be augmented by explicit simulations of the electrochemical interface, incorporating solvation and field effects using the computational hydrogen electrode and implicit solvent models, to understand potential-dependent behavior and kinetic barriers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, descriptor-based activity volcano for HOR, directly linking catalyst electronic structure to performance. It will quantify how interfacial fields modulate adsorption energies, offering a microscopic explanation for the high activity of Pt. The resulting framework will guide the computational screening and rational design of novel HOR catalysts with reduced precious-metal content.
proposals.novelty: While descriptor analysis is mature for reduction reactions (like ORR) and OER, its systematic application to HOR as an oxidation reaction, explicitly connecting it to the field-effect insights from recent OER studies, is novel. The proposal uniquely tests whether the conceptual tools developed for complex multi-electron oxidations (OER) can simplify and explain the seemingly simple two-electron HOR.

proposals.title: Discovery of Earth-Abundant Dual-Function Catalysts for Electrochemical Ammonia Synthesis via High-Throughput Computational Screening
proposals.motivation: The electrochemical nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) for ammonia synthesis is hindered by the competing hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and the scarcity of efficient catalysts. Platinum-group metals like Pt are known to have near-optimal hydrogen adsorption energies for HER, but their cost and poor NRR selectivity limit practical application. Identifying cheap, dual-function materials that can selectively activate nitrogen while managing proton adsorption is a critical, unsolved challenge for sustainable ammonia production.
proposals.hypothesis: By screening alloys and intermetallics of earth-abundant transition metals (e.g., Fe, Co, Ni, Mo) and early transition metal nitrides, we can identify materials where the adsorption energies of key NRR intermediates (e.g., *N, *NH) and *H are simultaneously tuned to be near their respective volcanic peaks, surpassing the single-function limitation of Pt.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations, using the BEEF-vdW functional for accurate adsorption energies, to construct a high-throughput computational screening pipeline. The search will focus on binary and ternary compositions, leveraging the Computational Materials Repository and scaling relations established in the Nørskov group. Adsorption energies for *N, *NH2, and *H will be calculated on high-symmetry surfaces. Machine learning models (e.g., graph neural networks) trained on initial DFT data will accelerate the identification of promising candidates with dual adsorption properties near the optimal targets derived from microkinetic models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a curated database of predicted earth-abundant catalysts with dual-function activity for NRR and managed HER. It will advance the fundamental understanding of descriptor-based design beyond single-adsorbate scaling relations. The top predicted materials will provide specific targets for experimental synthesis and testing in continuous-flow electrochemical reactors, directly informing the development of efficient ammonia electrosynthesis processes.
proposals.novelty: While previous searches often target a single optimal adsorption energy (e.g., *N for NRR), this proposal explicitly seeks materials that satisfy *two* adsorption energy constraints simultaneously, a non-trivial optimization problem. It moves beyond identifying Pt mimics for HER to finding new compositions that actively promote NRR while intelligently suppressing HER through tailored surface electronic structure.

ideas.title: Electrochemically Stabilized Single-Atom Alloy Catalysts via Sulfur Vacancy Engineering
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the use of electrochemically generated, stable sulfur vacancies in the basal plane of 2D TMDs (e.g., MoS2) as precise anchoring sites for single copper atoms, forming a novel class of single-atom alloy catalysts. The study will focus on how the electrochemical potential can be used not only to create these anchoring vacancies but also to maintain them under operational conditions, thereby stabilizing the dispersed Cu atoms against aggregation during demanding C-C coupling reactions like CO2 electroreduction to multi-carbon products. This approach synergistically combines atomic-scale site control from single-atom alloys with the dynamic stability mechanism of electrochemically managed defects, enabling a catalyst that can potentially be regenerated in-situ for unprecedented durability.

ideas.title: Microkinetic Modeling of Spillover-Controlled Thermal Catalysis for Predictive Selectivity Tuning
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the concept of using hydrogen spillover as a dynamic control knob in thermal catalysis with first-principles microkinetic modeling. The core approach is to calculate the activation barriers for all elementary steps on both the metal catalyst and the support/receptor site, then incorporate the flux of spilled-over hydrogen as a tunable variable within the microkinetic model. This allows for the simulation of product selectivity as a function of spillover efficiency, which is governed by catalyst-support interface design and conditions. The outcome is a predictive computational platform to identify catalyst architectures and operational windows that maximize desired products through controlled hydrogen delivery, bridging materials design with reactor performance.

ideas.title: Catalytic Coupling via Kinetic Trapping of Metastable Intermediates
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that more hydrogenated species are inherently more reactive in coupling reactions. By drawing an analogy from enzymatic catalysis—where enzymes achieve remarkable selectivity by stabilizing high-energy transition states and metastable intermediates—we propose to design synthetic catalytic systems that apply kinetic trapping. The adapted research idea is to engineer a catalyst (e.g., a bifunctional surface with specific defect sites, or a porous scaffold with precise confinement) that adsorbs or coordinates a less hydrogenated intermediate in a configuration that both accelerates its coupling and protects it from further hydrogenation. This creates a kinetic pathway where the coupling of the less hydrogenated species outcompetes its hydrogenation, inverting the standard reactivity trend and potentially enabling new selective routes to valuable coupled products like alkenes from alkynes or partially reduced biomass derivatives.

ideas.title: Catalytic C-C Coupling on Copper via Operando Surface 'Annealing' and Defect Mapping
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a perfect, static copper catalyst surface during electrochemical C-C coupling. By drawing an analogy from structural biology's shift to studying dynamic protein ensembles, the research adapts the solution of using time-resolved, operando techniques to capture non-equilibrium surface states. The adapted idea proposes to apply operando scanning probe microscopy and time-resolved X-ray spectroscopy to map the dynamic restructuring and defect evolution of a copper electrode under applied potential. The goal is to correlate this real-time 'surface folding' landscape—the creation and annihilation of steps, adatoms, and vacancies—with catalytic activity and selectivity for C-C bond formation, thereby identifying which transient, defective surface configurations are functionally essential.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Mapping of Hydrogenation State on Coupling Selectivity

A model catalytic system (e.g., Cu or Pd-based) will be used to study the coupling of aldehydes or alkenes under controlled hydrogen chemical potentials. Parallelized reactor testing with in-situ IR and Raman spectroscopy will correlate coupling yields with the spectroscopic fingerprints of surface species, while microkinetic models are iteratively refined to identify the specific conditions (e.g., coverage-driven changes in activation barriers) that cause less hydrogenated species to become the dominant coupling partners.

ideas.description: This research designs a stable single-atom catalyst (SAC) system to study C-C coupling by permanently locking isolated copper sites within a nitrogen-doped carbon scaffold. The catalyst is synthesized via the pyrolysis of a Cu-containing zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF), ensuring atomic dispersion and creating strong, thermally stable Cu-N4 coordination sites. This architecture prevents the aggregation of Cu atoms under reaction conditions, enabling the reliable study of structure-activity relationships. The electronic properties of the Cu center are further tuned by varying the pyrolysis temperature and introducing secondary heteroatoms (e.g., B, P) into the carbon matrix to optimize catalytic performance for target coupling reactions.

ideas.description: This research direction integrates the dynamic coverage effects from C-C coupling with the inverse problem of C-C bond scission during electro-oxidation. It develops a single computational and theoretical framework to map how fluctuating local coverages of intermediates (e.g., *CO, *CHx, *OH) simultaneously and bidirectionally modulate the activation barriers for both C-C bond formation and cleavage on the same catalyst surface. This allows for the first-principles identification of catalyst materials and operational conditions (potential, flux) that maximize selectivity for desired multi-carbon products during CO2/CO reduction while simultaneously minimizing their subsequent deactivation or unselective oxidation in fuel cell modes, addressing a critical stability-selectivity trade-off.

ideas.title: Adapting Packed-Bed Reactor Design for Uniform Electrochemical Desulfurization
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from chemical reaction engineering, specifically the design of large-scale packed-bed reactors for heterogeneous catalysis. The analogous problem of achieving uniform reaction conditions across a bulk solid catalyst is solved through careful engineering of flow distribution and bed structure. The adapted research idea is to design and test a continuous-flow electrochemical reactor where MoS2 catalyst powder is contained within a conductive packed-bed electrode. By adapting flow distributors, baffle systems, and electrode geometries from catalytic reactor design, the goal is to scale up the electrochemical desulfurization process while maintaining uniform electrolyte access and current distribution, preventing localized over- or under-processing of the MoS2 material.

ideas.title: Mapping Nanoparticle Morphology to Reaction Pathways via Catalytic Descriptor Landscapes
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from computational biochemistry's protein-ligand docking problems, where descriptor-based landscapes predict binding outcomes. The analogous problem of predicting catalytic selectivity based on nanoparticle structure is solved by constructing a catalytic descriptor landscape. For a given copper nanoparticle size and shape, key electronic and geometric descriptors (generalized coordination number, d-band center, facet-specific adsorption energies) will be computed using DFT. These descriptors for a vast morphology library will be used as input features for a machine learning model trained on DFT-calculated energy barriers for competing C-C coupling pathways. The output is a multi-dimensional landscape that visually and quantitatively maps how specific morphological features steer the reaction toward ethylene vs. ethanol precursors, providing a rational design principle for catalyst synthesis.

ideas.title: Adapting Allosteric Control from Enzymes to Single-Atom Alloy Catalysts
ideas.description: This idea applies the principle of allosteric control from enzymology to the design of single-atom alloy (SAA) catalysts for C-C coupling. In enzymes, a binding event at a regulatory site remotely modulates the active site's geometry and electronics. The adapted solution is to engineer the host metal matrix surrounding a single copper atom with deliberate 'regulatory' features—such as vacancies, secondary dopants, or engineered strain fields at a defined nanoscale distance. By applying external stimuli (e.g., electrochemical potential, specific ligand adsorption) to these regulatory features, the electronic and geometric state of the isolated Cu site can be tuned dynamically, offering a novel pathway to control the selectivity and rate of C-C coupling reactions with precision akin to biological catalysis.

ideas.title: Dynamic sulfur buffering via sacrificial scaffolds for intrinsic HER catalyst stabilization
ideas.description: This study investigates a preventive stabilization strategy for transition metal sulfide HER catalysts by controlling the chemical potential of sulfur in their immediate microenvironment. Instead of attempting to re-sulfurize degraded catalysts, the approach designs composite materials where a secondary, sacrificial phase (e.g., a metallic sulfide with higher sulfur affinity) acts as a dynamic sulfur buffer. This buffer continuously absorbs sulfur species leaching from the active catalyst, thereby kinetically and thermodynamically inhibiting the formation of detrimental sulfur vacancies. The research will correlate the sulfur-buffering capacity and kinetics of the scaffold with the operational stability and electronic structure of the active catalyst under HER conditions.

ideas.title: Bayesian Inverse Problem Framework for Potential-Dependent C-C Cleavage Pathways in Electro-Oxidation
ideas.description: This research establishes a closed-loop, computational-experimental framework to solve the inverse problem of identifying the potential-dependent elementary steps and kinetics for hydrocarbon C-C bond breaking in fuel cells. A forward microkinetic model, informed by DFT calculations of adsorbate binding and reaction barriers on model Pt and Pt-alloy surfaces, serves as the core. The inverse solver employs Bayesian inference to fit this model to synchronized operando DEMS and IR spectroscopy data, quantifying the uncertainty in extracted rate constants and dominant reaction pathways as a function of applied potential. This approach moves beyond correlative observation to a rigorous, quantitative deconvolution of the complex reaction network governing electro-oxidation efficiency and catalyst poisoning.

ideas.title: Dual-System Thermal Catalysis with Programmable Hydrogen Spillover via a Chemical Relay
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from synthetic biology's use of programmable chemical relays to shuttle intermediates between metabolic pathways. The analogous solution involves decoupling a robust initiation step from a specialized transformation step to gain independent control. Adapted to thermal catalysis, the research proposes a dual-system where hydrogen activation is separated from hydrogenation. A primary catalyst dissociates H2, and a tunable, chemical 'spillover bridge' (like a reducible oxide layer or a molecular organic shuttle) acts as a programmable relay to control the flux of spilled-over hydrogen atoms to a secondary catalyst site. This allows for real-time variation of hydrogenation activity and selectivity in purely thermal conditions, analogous to varying an electrochemical potential.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Electro-oxidation of Hydrocarbons via Proteolytic Enzyme Analogy
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical framework from biochemistry to study C-C bond breaking in fuel cell oxidation. The analogous problem is how proteases selectively cleave peptide bonds, which is solved by tracing bond scission with isotopic labels and probing active site function with controlled mutations. Adapted to electro-oxidation, the research would use isotopic tracing of carbon atoms in hydrocarbons during oxidation, combined with operando analysis and controlled catalyst surface engineering, to solve the inverse problem of mapping the sequence and active sites responsible for specific C-C bond cleavage pathways. This approach aims to reveal the 'catalytic mechanism' of bond breaking on electrode surfaces.

ideas.title: AIMD Simulation of Electrolyte Interactions with Mechanically-Defective MoS2 Interfaces
ideas.description: This research direction combines mechanical defect generation via ball milling with ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulation of solvation. The core idea is to use AIMD to model the explicit liquid electrolyte interface not with pristine, but with mechanically pre-defected MoS2 surfaces, where sulfur vacancies and edge sites are explicitly introduced to mimic the post-milling state. This allows for the first-principles investigation of how these non-equilibrium defect sites alter interfacial ion ordering, solvation structure, and charge transfer kinetics in real time. The synergy provides a predictive computational framework to understand and optimize the mechanochemical pretreatment of electrode materials for applications in batteries or electrocatalysis.

ideas.title: Operando spectroscopic validation of coverage-dependent C-C coupling energetics
ideas.description: This project integrates operando surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) with grand-canonical DFT to directly test and refine the hypothesis that local adsorbate coverage dynamically dictates C-C coupling barriers. A model electrochemical reactor with a nanostructured electrode enables spatially-resolved, real-time measurement of local CO or hydrocarbon intermediate coverages under varying potentials and current densities. These experimental coverage data directly parameterize and validate multi-scale simulations that compute potential- and coverage-dependent activation barriers, closing the loop between theory and experiment to build a predictive, microkinetic model of selectivity control.

ideas.title: Predictive Microkinetic Mapping of CO-to-Product Networks
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the calculation of full reaction networks from CO to specific hydrocarbons (e.g., ethylene) with the rigorous validation of theoretical barriers via microkinetic modeling. The synergy lies in using the microkinetic model as an active diagnostic tool to analyze the complete network: it simulates product distributions under realistic conditions, identifies which calculated barriers most significantly control selectivity toward the desired product, and pinpoints the specific elementary steps where theoretical uncertainty most critically impacts the predicted outcome. This creates a targeted feedback loop where computational effort is focused on refining the DFT calculations for only the most kinetically sensitive steps in the pathway, ultimately enabling the predictive design of catalysts to steer selectivity.

ideas.title: Morphology-Dependent Sulfur Vacancy Dynamics and Re-incorporation in Copper Sulfide Nanoparticles for Stable C-C Coupling
ideas.description: This research investigates the interplay between the size and shape of copper sulfide nanoparticles and the dynamics of sulfur vacancies during electrocatalytic C-C coupling reactions. It aims to determine how specific morphologies influence both the dominant reaction pathway and the propensity for vacancy formation and migration, which drive catalyst degradation. The core innovation is to use controlled, morphology-dependent re-sulfurization as an in-situ diagnostic tool to map vacancy landscapes and as a potential mitigation strategy. This approach seeks to rationally design nanoparticle morphologies that optimize catalytic selectivity while inherently resisting deactivation through engineered vacancy stability or facile self-healing.

ideas.title: Closed-loop electrochemical synthesis with operando monitoring for controlled and stable S-vacancy generation in MoS2
ideas.description: This project develops a scalable electrochemical reactor system that uses operando optical spectroscopy to monitor S-vacancy formation in MoS2 catalysts in real-time. The spectroscopic signal feeds into a control algorithm that dynamically adjusts the electrochemical parameters to achieve a target, uniform vacancy density across the electrode. Immediately following vacancy generation, an in-situ electrochemical passivation treatment is applied to stabilize the defective structure. This integrated approach ensures precise control, reproducibility, and enhanced durability of the S-vacancies, meeting the rigorous demands of industrial catalyst manufacturing.

ideas.title: Dynamic Solvation-Coverage Coupling in Electrochemical C-C Bond Formation
ideas.description: This research direction integrates explicit solvation and electrolyte modeling with dynamic, fluctuating adsorbate coverage to study C-C coupling in electrochemical environments. It investigates how the local density and arrangement of adsorbates (e.g., CO*) directly influence the structure of the electric double layer, the orientation of solvent molecules, and the specific adsorption of ions at the electrode interface. Crucially, this altered solvation shell then feeds back to modify the lateral interactions between adsorbates and the stability of key transition states, creating a coverage-dependent solvation environment. The core objective is to map this coupled dynamic landscape to identify conditions where solvation-coverage synergy either dramatically suppresses or promotes C-C coupling rates.

ideas.title: Adapting Metabolic Pathway Reconstruction for CO-to-Hydrocarbon Reaction Networks
ideas.description: This idea proposes to map complete reaction networks from CO to specific products like ethylene or ethanol by drawing a direct analogy from systems biology. In metabolic engineering, the problem of finding all biochemical pathways to a target molecule is solved by reconstructing an organism's full metabolic network from its genome and using computational tools like Flux Balance Analysis to calculate viable flux pathways. We adapt this solution to catalysis by constructing an analogous, comprehensive network of surface reactions (a 'catalome') for CO conversion on relevant catalytic materials. By applying similar graph-based and constraint-based computational frameworks to this network, the research would systematically identify, enumerate, and evaluate all possible reaction pathways leading to a desired hydrocarbon, providing a complete mechanistic map to guide catalyst design and process optimization.

ideas.title: Operando Decoupling of Structural and Chemical Degradation in Electrochemically Generated S-Vacancies
ideas.description: This study investigates the deactivation mechanisms of electrochemically created basal plane S-vacancies by distinguishing between structural vacancy annihilation and chemical site poisoning. An operando setup simultaneously tracks the vacancy concentration via spectroscopic signatures and the catalytic activity of the vacancies via model reactions. By using isotopically labeled electrolytes and controlled atmosphere cells, the work identifies the specific chemical species (e.g., O*, C*, OH*) responsible for passivation. The outcome is a mechanistic degradation map that informs strategies, such as electrolyte engineering or protective overlayers, to preserve vacancy functionality rather than just their structural existence.

ideas.title: Probe Transient Basal Plane Activation in 2H-MoS2 via Reversible Electronic Perturbation
ideas.description: This research rigorously tests the hypothesis of latent basal plane activity in 2H-MoS2 by avoiding permanent structural defects. The approach uses a dual-probe experimental system: a microreactor for electrochemical measurements integrated with an optoelectronic platform capable of applying in-situ tunable strain, electrostatic doping via a solid gate dielectric, and sub-bandgap optical excitation. The key is to measure catalytic current exclusively during the application of these reversible perturbations, which modulate charge density and band structure, and to confirm the complete return to a vacancy-free, pristine state after their removal. This method definitively establishes whether activity is an inducible, transient electronic property or an intrinsic feature requiring permanent lattice defects.

ideas.title: Electrochemical Bone Grafting: In Situ Sulfur Replenishment for Catalyst Regeneration
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from biomedical bone tissue regeneration, where controlled ion release is used to fill structural defects, to the problem of sulfur vacancy degradation in HER catalysts. The analogous solution involves implantable scaffolds that provide a sustained, local source of mineralization ions. Adapted to electrochemistry, the research would study the design of an electrolyte system or catalyst support that provides a controlled, in situ source of sulfur. The goal is to enable the continuous, mild re-sulfurization of catalyst vacancies as they form during hydrogen evolution, mimicking a self-healing, 'grafting' mechanism to understand and actively mitigate catalyst degradation, scaling the solution from material design to system-level electrolyte management.

ideas.title: Stabilizing Electrochemical S-Vacancies via Surface Passivation and Dopant Pinning
ideas.description: This idea investigates the long-term operational stability of basal plane S-vacancies generated via electrochemical desulfurization by adapting a solution from semiconductor device physics. In semiconductors, electrically active defects are stabilized through passivation (e.g., hydrogen termination) and dopant pinning to prevent migration. Adapted to this context, the research will characterize stability by exploring two stabilization pathways: 1) post-electrochemical passivation of vacancy sites with controlled doses of atomic hydrogen or oxygen to satisfy dangling bonds, and 2) the electrochemical co-creation of vacancy-dopant complexes, where substitutional dopant atoms (like Re for Mo or Se for S) kinetically 'pin' nearby vacancies, suppressing their diffusion and annihilation. The work will compare the electrochemical activity and temporal decay of vacancy concentration for passivated, pinned, and untreated vacancies to identify strategies for durable defect engineering.

ideas.title: Programmable vacancy arrays to probe adsorbate coverage-dependent C-C coupling dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction uses scanning probe lithography to fabricate precisely defined, nanoscale arrays of sulfur vacancies on a transition metal dichalcogenide surface. These vacancy arrays serve as a tunable template to systematically control and vary the local coverage of carbon-containing adsorbates (e.g., CHx fragments) by acting as preferential binding sites. The core investigation then employs in-situ spectroscopic and scanning probe measurements to quantify how these engineered, non-uniform coverage profiles dynamically alter the activation barriers and pathways for subsequent C-C coupling reactions. This approach uniquely enables a direct, causal test of coverage-dependent reactivity models with spatial resolution unattainable on conventional uniform surfaces.

ideas.title: Adapting Grand Canonical Monte Carlo's Chemical Potential Control to Grand Canonical DFT for Electrode Potential Control
ideas.description: The original idea proposes applying constant-potential DFT methods to directly model variable electrode potential in electrochemical systems. By analogy to Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) methods in computational statistical mechanics and adsorption science—where the chemical potential (μ) of a species is held constant by allowing particle exchange with a reservoir—we can adapt this conceptual framework to electronic structure. In GCMC, the intensive variable μ is controlled, and the extensive variable (particle number N) fluctuates. For an electrode, the analogous intensive variable is the electrochemical potential (Fermi level, tied to electrode potential), and the extensive variable is the number of electrons. The adapted research idea is to develop a Grand Canonical DFT formalism that incorporates an explicit electron reservoir at a fixed Fermi level. The simulation cell (representing the electrode/electrolyte interface) would exchange electrons with this reservoir, allowing the total electron count to fluctuate dynamically during the DFT self-consistent field procedure to maintain a constant, user-defined electrode potential. This would provide a first-principles, atomistically detailed method for simulating electrochemical systems under true potentiostatic (constant potential) conditions, a significant advancement over current standard approaches using fixed-charge supercells.

ideas.title: Dynamic coverage effects on C-C coupling: A reaction-diffusion adaptation
ideas.description: This research challenges the constant adsorbate coverage assumption in C-C coupling by drawing an analogy from population ecology. In predator-prey systems, population densities dynamically affect interaction rates through reaction-diffusion equations (Lotka-Volterra models). We adapt this approach to heterogeneous catalysis by treating adsorbates as dynamic populations that diffuse across the catalyst surface while undergoing reactions. The research will develop coverage-dependent kinetic models where C-C coupling barriers become functions of local, time-varying adsorbate concentrations, enabling prediction of how coverage gradients and transient depletion zones affect selectivity and activity in complex reaction networks.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Quantified Microkinetic Modeling for Robust Theoretical Barrier Validation
ideas.description: This approach couples high-throughput computational catalysis with a structured uncertainty quantification protocol to assess the reliability of predicted product distributions. The system automatically computes reaction barriers and energies across a ensemble of validated density functionals and methodological settings, generating a distribution of possible kinetic parameters. These parameter distributions are then fed into a Bayesian microkinetic model, which outputs likelihoods for various product selectivity outcomes. This allows researchers to distinguish predictions that are robust across theoretical methods from those that are highly sensitive to computational choices, providing a statistically rigorous validation of theoretical barriers against experimental data.

ideas.title: Adapting Mechanochemical Polymer Scission for Defect Engineering in 2D Materials
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from polymer chemistry, where mechanical force is applied to induce controlled chain scission. The analogous solution uses high-shear mechanical processing like ball milling to break polymer chains, creating reactive ends and modifying material properties. Adapted to this research context, the principle is applied to the 2D material MoS2. By precisely controlling the milling parameters (energy, duration, atmosphere, and ball-to-powder ratio), the mechanical forces of ball milling can be tuned not just for grinding, but for the deliberate 'scissoring' of the MoS2 layers. This process controllably generates specific defects—such as sulfur vacancies, edge sites, and nanopores—through mechanical force, transforming ball milling from a simple size-reduction method into a scalable, tunable technique for defect engineering in bulk transition metal dichalcogenides.

ideas.title: Apply Dip-Pen Nanolithography for Programmable S-Vacancy Patterning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from molecular biology's Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)—a scanning probe technique used to fabricate DNA microarrays by directly writing molecular 'inks'—to the problem of creating precise S-vacancy patterns. In the analogous field, an AFM tip deposits different biomolecular inks with nanoscale control to create complex, programmable features. Adapted to this context, the AFM tip would be coated with a chemical agent designed to selectively remove sulfur atoms from a 2D material substrate (e.g., MoS2) upon controlled contact. This method transfers the DPN capability for direct-write, multi-feature patterning to create custom, non-periodic arrays of vacancies. It enables fundamental studies of vacancy interactions in designed geometries and the exploration of vacancy-based nanoscale devices, offering greater flexibility than standard lithographic vacancy patterning.

ideas.title: Integrated SPM Lithography-Spectroscopy for Verified S-Vacancy Array Fabrication and Characterization
ideas.description: This approach combines atomic force microscopy (AFM) based lithography with in situ scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS) within a single ultra-high vacuum system to create and directly verify nanoscale sulfur vacancy arrays. The conductive probe first performs voltage-pulsing lithography on a transition metal dichalcogenide surface to generate patterned regions. Immediately after patterning, the same tip is used for STS mapping to detect the distinct electronic signatures of sulfur vacancies, confirming their successful creation and spatial distribution. This closed-loop, verified fabrication platform enables reliable fundamental studies by guaranteeing that the measured electronic, optical, or chemical properties are definitively linked to the engineered vacancy arrays.
